> are we in trouble when we inevitably forget to update these versions? I suppose not?

```python

but if we remove `{features.Mask: 0}` then mask will be also padded with 123. So, to get correct behaviour users would need to do:
```python
transform = transforms.Pad(1, fill={features.Image: 123, features.Mask: 0}, padding_mode="constant")
```

> Shouldn't this also raise an InvalidCheckValue(f'Invalid key {key}.') ? Perhaps more simply:

Yes, your solution would be simpler if we want it to fail always, also when checking for the last nested key, but I thought it would make sense to not, let me explain it with an example:
```
        resource_type=PIDRelation(
            ...
            value_check=dict(props=dict(relation_type='depositable')),
        )
```
In this case we will check for the value { props: relation_type: 'depositable' } in the `resource_type` vocabulary values, so if instead of `props` we write `props1` we want it to fail, but if we don't have the `relation_type` defined in the vocabulary value, I consider it standard so we don't need to check for the value and it's accepted.

> In keeping with original there is no recursion on lists (is that desired?).

Regarding the lists, there is no recursion, I thought it could be interesting to accept a list of values like this:
```
        resource_type=PIDRelation(
            ...
            value_check=dict(props=dict(relation_type=['depositable', 'linkable'])),
        )
```
This way we check if the vocabulary value matches with one of the values of the list to accept it.

> Although to be honest, it's a bit of a custom verification setup... why do we need it?

Is needed basically for the resource_type vocabularies to be able to differentiate between linkable and depositable values (and any other similar cases we may have)

While it seems we can emulate `GetCurrentThreadStackLimits` via [`TIB`](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Win32_Thread_Information_Block), we should use `DeallocationStack` (not 
`StackLimit`) for the real stack address. The implementation `GetCurrentThreadStackLimits` does the same (x64 implementation):
```
mov         r8,qword ptr gs:[30h]      // get TIB
mov         rax,qword ptr [r8+1478h]   // TIB + 1478h is DeallocationStack
mov         qword ptr [rcx],rax        // return LowLimit
mov         rax,qword ptr [r8+8]       // TIB + 8h is StackBase
mov         qword ptr [rdx],rax        // return HighLimit
ret
```
So, on x86_32 the `DeallocationStack` offset will be another and it seems the current approach is the lesser of two evils.

Thank you very much for the comment guys.

I spent quite a lot of time the past week trying to investigate on how to make this configuration as generic as possible, experimenting with several projects, but unfortunately I could not find that "one configuration to rule them all".

There seem to be a lot of factors that can influence the report, for example:

- flat vs src-layout
- In-package tests vs separated `tests` folder
- Usage of `develop=True` in `tox.ini`
- Usage of `isolated_build=True` in `tox.ini`
- Usage of `changedir` in `tox.ini`
- Usage of `--installpkg` flag with `tox`
- `coverage` vs `pytest-cov`

Some things I have noticed:

- When using a flat layout and `develop=True`, omitting the `[paths]` seems to work OK. However that does not work well with src-layout...
- When I was experimenting with `tempora`, for some reason, using `--cov` alone seems to cause coverage warnings, while using `--cov tempora` results in a cleaner output, **regardless** of the presence of `[run] source = tempora` in `.coveragerc`.

So far the best approach I did manage to find (less noisy) is to use `[run] source = ...`, `[paths]` and still pass in the command line `--cov=PACKAGE_NAME` instead of just `--cov`. That seems to work for a broad combination of the factors I described previously, but it does need the name of the package under test to be hardcoded in some configuration files...

@hroncok that initially confused me as well, but the difference is that `\n` is a valid character (newline), so python will not make any effort to escape it during `repr` calls.  For example:

```python
>>> a = 'C:\what\not'
>>> a
'C:\\what\not'
>>> print(a)
C:\what
ot
```

The goal of using raw strings is a bit disingenuous here and a bit of a shortcut, the "real" fix would be to just posix-ify these paths with `pathlib.Path().as_posix()` calls like we do everywhere else (with an import exception which falls back to `pathlib2` for python 2 if we want to avoid just using `vistir.compat.Path`).

Please consider the following example:
```
def mmap(fd, offset, size):
    if size & 0xfff != 0:
        size = (size & ~0xfff) + 0x1000
    assert size > 0

    aligned_offset = offset & ~0xfff
    print("mmap_function(0, %#x, prot, flags, fd, %#x)" % (size, aligned_offset))
    result = 0x12340000 # page aligned address returned by mmap()
    return result + offset - aligned_offset

def munmap(address, size):
    aligned_address = address & ~0xfff
    print("munmap_function(%#x, %#x)" % (aligned_address, size))

offset = 0x1
size = 0x10

address = mmap(0, offset, size)
print("result is %#x" % address)
munmap(address, size)
```

and the corresponding output:
```
mmap_function(0, 0x1000, prot, flags, fd, 0x0)
result is 0x12340001
munmap_function(0x12340000, 0x10)
```

We're trying to map 0x10 bytes from a file starting at offset 0x1. You can see that we end up calling mmap() with a offset of 0 and a size of 0x1000. The address returned by mmap() is the address at which the beginning of the file is mapped, so we need to add 0x1 to the pointer returned to the user.

When the user tries to free the memory using this pointer, we align it back to recover the value returned by mmap(). We can use the original size because munmap() will unmap the whole page.

Yes, that's a good point. I considered using `ImportError`, but re-throwing `ImportError` with a different message (having in mind this exception is used (almost?) exclusively by the `import` statement) seems wrong. Or at least not "obvious way to do it".

Due to exception nesting, user will be able to see the whole exception stack trace anyway, and see that the `RuntimeError` was caused by a failing import.

As a Python user I would be surprised to see `ImportError` raised because of another `ImportError` (like `imp` or `importlib` failed importing something...)

If you're concerned about handling this case programmatically, then subclassing (of `ImportError`) would probably be the best way. But I don't think we should overthink this because dimod might very well become dependency of the client in future.

Correct. As noted in the C++ class documentation, there are currently two cases where a ManagedArray can be invalidated: 
```
There are two specific scenarios when ManagedArrays are invalidated.
    1. ManagedArrays in state 2 will be invalidated if the data source
       array is deleted.
    2. ManagedArrays in state 3 will be invalidated if the data source is
        deleted or changes in size.
```

That's true not just for deallocating `m_size`, but also the underlying `m_data` pointer to the array itself. If we do what I suggested in my longer comment and stop using this object as a pointer to NumPy data, then we could write this class as pure C++ and use smart pointers to alleviate this issue. Currently the problem is that trying to point to a numpy.ndarray means that we can't use smart pointers without adding other complexity because they'll delete the data even if the numpy array should be owning it. If we stop pointing to numpy arrays some of these problems go away.

I believe that "Port XXX Zombie. Reset the port" and "Buffer is being invalidated, segment_size may be insufficient" are because we exit the applications with Ctrl+C with some unclosed DDS resources.

- Ports 7412 and 7413 are default port for meta-traffic unicast for domain 0, first participant. "Port XXX Zombie. Reset the port" appear for each domain that the sniffer previously ran in, even after restart, so I think there are some files that indicate it is open.
- "Buffer is being invalidated, segment_size may be insufficient" is issued when trying to allocate shared memory buffer for the first time (with discovery message) and seeing that buffers where allocated on a previous run and are unused anymore.

I am not fully sure why "Attempting to add existing writer" is issued, I think it is because we use both shared memory and TCPv4 transports on same host (It is issued for a topic discovery message)

Here are the results of measurements for 200,000 rows DataFrame. It seems that broadcasting is significantly faster rather than `to_pandas()`
```
MEASURING single-partition columns; df shape is (200000, 255)
to_pandas 2.890704656019807
broadcasting 0.8330112155526876


MEASURING multi-partition columns; df shape is (200000, 253)
to_pandas 4.838804990053177
broadcasting 1.0249458067119122
```

<details><summary>script</summary>


```python
import numpy as np
import os
import pandas
from timeit import default_timer as timer


def generate_data_file(
    filename=None,
    nrows=200000,
    ncols=256,
    multiline_rows=False,
    force=False,
    rand_low=-100,
    rang_high=100,
    log=True,
):
    if filename is None:
        filename = os.path.abspath(
            f"int_dataset-{nrows},{ncols},{rand_low},{rang_high},{multiline_rows}.csv"
        )

    print("generating", filename)

    if os.path.exists(filename) and not force:
        return filename

    data = {
        f"col{i}": np.concatenate(
            [
                np.concatenate(
                    [
                        ["some\nvery very very\nlong string\nwith many multilines"],
                        np.random.randint(rand_low, rang_high, 9),
                    ]
                )
                for _ in np.arange(ncols // 10)
            ]
        )
        if (i % 10 == 0 and multiline_rows)
        else list(np.random.randint(rand_low, rang_high, nrows // 10)) * 10
        for i in np.arange(ncols)
    }
    print("dict generated!")
    df = pandas.DataFrame(data)
    print("dataframe created!")
    df.to_csv(filename)
    print("csv ready!")
    return filename


def measure_time(md_df, columns, test_label):
    subframe = md_df[columns]
    md_df = md_df.drop(columns=columns)

    print(f"\n\nMEASURING {test_label}; df shape is {md_df.shape}")

    t1 = timer()
    pd_subframe = subframe._to_pandas()

    def single(df):
        pd_subframe.max()
        df.max()
        return df

    result = pd.DataFrame(
        query_compiler=PandasQueryCompiler(
            md_df._query_compiler._modin_frame._apply_full_axis(0, single)
        )
    )
    repr(result)
    print("to_pandas", timer() - t1)

    t1 = timer()

    def single(df, other):
        other.max()
        df.max()
        return df

    result = pd.DataFrame(
        query_compiler=PandasQueryCompiler(
            md_df._query_compiler._modin_frame.broadcast_apply_full_axis(
                0, single, other=subframe._query_compiler._modin_frame
            )
        )
    )
    repr(result)
    print("broadcasting", timer() - t1)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    import modin.pandas as pd
    from modin.backends import PandasQueryCompiler
    from modin.pandas.test.utils import df_equals, test_data
    import pandas
    import numpy as np

    filename = generate_data_file()

    md_df = pd.read_csv(filename)

    measure_time(md_df, md_df.columns[:2], test_label="single-partition columns")
    measure_time(
        md_df,
        [*md_df.columns[:2], *md_df.columns[-2:]],
        test_label="multi-partition columns",
    )

```

I was thinking a little more about this while walking the dog.  Looking at uses of `load`, `query` and `scan`, I see the following kinds of conditions:

 * things that are used to generate partitionKey / rowKey
 * datestamps (`expires: Entity.op.greaterThanOrEqual(new Date())`)
 * problematic cases
   * `storageType: 'blob'` in `services/queue/src/api.js` -- we can in fact delete this code, as we no longer support blobs
   * `resolveTask` in `services/queue/src/dependencytracker.js` -- we can change this around to just fetch all dependencies of the task (omitting the condition) and check the property with an `if` statement in the JS code.  In this particular case, that won't be especially inefficient.

So, I think we can eliminate the two problematic cases and *only* build conditions for the first two cases -- that is, the only WHERE clause elements we should ever build dynamically are for datestamp properties, where we are given a Date object in JS and can properly convert that to a SQL string without risk of SQL injection.

I don't think you really need on_evaluate_step() callback, because you have access to the worker in on_episode_step(), which you can use to decide whether this is an evaluation episode or a normal rollout episode.
it's actually a bit weird to only call on_evaluate_step() for eval episodes. because an episode step is an episode step, if someone wants to modify an episode step in any way, the modification needs to be applied to both rollout and eval episodes.
so my suggestion is to only add the on_evaluate_start() and on_evaluate_end() callbacks, and leverage the existing on_episode_step() to do what you want.

1) norm_v_mp = norm_v_ff * norm_r_oc , norm_i_mp = norm_i_ff * norm_r_sc
Line 2 is only approximate (to about 0.2%)
2) stack_v_mp ~ stack_v_ff + stack_r_oc , stack_i_mp ~ stack_i_ff + stack_r_sc

We are taking accurate multiplicative losses and the putting them onto a linear graph so there may be some small distortion but it is small. 

It's the overall relative losses (e.g. r_oc vs. r_sc) and any changes with time that matter. The sum of all the stack losses and the product of all multiplicative losses are the same from stack_6 and stack_4 as that's the way they drop from 1/ff to pr_dc and the stack graphs are very useful.

That's probably 2/3 true most of the complexity comes from `_unwrap_single_value`, but part of it comes from trying to mirror that same behavior in `ModelChain`. I snuck an optional kwarg into the decorator that can prevent unwrapping (`unwrap=False`), but we would then need to unwrap inside `ModelChain` to maintain the same behavior when users want `ModelChain.dc` for a single array system.

The other problem with the "unwrap" parameter (and with changing the type passed around internally) is that because the ModelChain tests are built with mocks we would need to change at least some of the existing tests to handle the difference in how the `PVSystem` functions are called.

It does have the potential to be awkward in user code, but I think the typical use case is that you are modeling a specific system so the type will be consistent within your code. I'm not sure of a way around that as long as we want to maintain the "legacy" single-array behavior.

This way it's only done once, and it's easier to see how changes to core.py impact performance.

Agreed in C at least, where you get the command-line arguments as arguments to `main()`. Storing them globally would be weird and unexpected there.

In Python, the command-line arguments are never implicitly passed to any startup function, and are accessed at `sys.argv` instead (have to `import sys` to get access to them). `sys.argv` immediately says "the command-line arguments" in idiomatic Python. `sys.argv` is kinda in the same class as `os.environ`, which holds the environment.

Think it comes down to whether the code ever needs to deal with anything but the command-line arguments. It would be simpler if it didn't.

The 0.15 CLI crashes when presented with a one-colon *component* resource type: https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/issues/1880. Customers have already been broken - I don't think it makes sense to fear a breaking change when the damage has already been done.

https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/issues/1880 also illustrates why I think it's dangerous to allow invalid `tokens.Type` to flow through our system - it is *incredibly easy* to slip up and forget to check if a resource is custom before attempting to pull packages out of a type. Yes, this change only changes what policy is updated with global vars.

The problem: 
- In order to update a policy, if it isn't in the memory cache it will need to be loaded from disk.
- Looking through the project, it looks like every time `set_global_vars()` is called `_get_global_vars` is passed as argument
- I'm not sure what kind of global vars there usually are, but looking into `rllib/execution/common.py` it looks like `_get_global_vars()` basically always returns an updated timestep
- Consequently, if any policy is written to disk, these are read from disk all the time to update their timesteps and we have constant disk access again, slowing everything down, kind of defeating the reason why we put them to the disk in the first place.

The solution:
- What at least helps with this problem is only updating global vars for policies that are being trained.
- If all policies that are being trained fit into the in-memory cache, the disk won't constantly be accessed.
- If they don't fit, then I see no way to stop constant disk access anyway.
- I'm not 100% sure this doesn't have other consequences, someone with better knowledge of the code base would have to judge that. However, I see no reason why policies that are not being trained would need their vars updated (are there even other vars besides timesteps).
- At any rate, without this case we are again at a point where any disk that is being offloaded to disk is constantly being accessed, making it basically unusable to have more policies than the `policy_map_capacity`.

Let's make the prioritization crystal clear in the docstring and in the line comments. I think it's especially important to justify the prioritization:

1. If we have a new trial to run, we want to prioritize PG_READY futures so it can be scheduled as soon as possible
2. If a PG is already ready (e.g. though PG caching), we immediately return with PG_READY
3. Otherwise (if there is no new trial to run), we shuffle existing futures randomly so that we don't prioritize early trials over late trials in results processing.

I like the isolation and code reuse. I'm in favour of that. I'm suggesting that the coupling be even looser than it is, rather than tighter.

I'm suggesting roughly that instead of

            qs = BulkApiQueryStep(obj, {}, self, query)

It would delegate to a subtask more like this:

            BulkApiQueryTask({output_file=my_csv_tempfile_path, query=query})

...

           BulkApiDmltask({operation = Operation.HARD_DELETE, id_csv=my_csv_tempfile_path})

Although the actual syntax might not be so clean unless we make a special helper for spawning sub-tasks. Which we probably could and should. I could have used it here:

https://github.com/SFDO-Tooling/CumulusCI/blob/4cf572a6703b7c3244bfd7514ad2efc24b1e5162/cumulusci/tasks/bulkdata/generate_and_load_data.py#L144

That would mean, among other things, that I could generate my own QueryResults file on the command line (using whatever complicated Python code or spreadsheet hackery I want) and then I could also pass it to a DmlStep to do the actual deleting (or Loading, or Updating). This would give CCI a user-accessible mechanism for deleting a list of IDs (wherever they came from) and *on top of that* a mechanism for deleting from a query, if the list of objects I want to delete can be expressed easily that way. (just this week I ran into a case where I had to write custom Python code to delete a list of objects that was not easily expressible as a simple query result).

Another thing it would mean is that the end user could try and redo a DmlTask if it fails (as long as they have a way of generating the list of records to delete, insert or update).

If search is successful, the return value is a 3-element tuple (file, pathname, description):

If the module does not live in a file, the returned file is None, pathname is the empty string, and the description tuple contains empty strings for its suffix and mode; the module type is indicated as given in parentheses above. If the search is unsuccessful, ImportError is raised. Other exceptions indicate problems with the arguments or environment.

If the module is a package, file is None, pathname is the package path and the last item in the description tuple is PKG_DIRECTORY.

I think we need to be careful here in the cache handling. I see that if you have this `state.joined` flag set, the cache doesn't save any of the parameters from the tensor table entry that are not normally stored within the responses (`device`, `dtype`, `shape`), since you know that the tensors won't be in the table. This is tricky because it now puts the cache in an inconsistent state across workers (for a particular response, some workers will have the additional tensor information, while others will not). We need to make sure that when the join operation is concluded, the cache is in a globally consistent state across all workers. 

For example, will this work in the following scenarios? (assume there are two ranks, either joining or submitting tensor A, each tuple is one set of operations submitted):
1. (join, A), (join, join) (A, A)
2. (A, A), (join, A), (join, join), (A, A)

The difference between the two cases is that in the first case, worker 0 does not have a preexisting cache entry for A, while in case 2, worker 0 does have a prexisting cached entry.

Note: I don't care enough about this issue to argue beyond basically this comment, so don't really mind if this gets ignored, but wanted to explain my thinking :)

My concern is that it may lead to implicit dependencies which aren't reflected in the build graph. Right now, I rely on BUILD files being correct in order to generate a deployable binary. And I can trust that when I run other tasks; if I write a task which checks for unused dependencies, or transitive deps that should be direct deps, or looks up the licences of my 3rdparty deps, or whatever, I can trust that Pants can statically analyse the transitive closure of deps (either because they're in a BUILD file, or because they're synthetically injected, or potentially because of dependency inference), and know that I have complete information to operate from.

The only point I can see of being able to specify multiple BFAs is to say "I know I don't say in a BUILD file that I don't depend on a logging implementation (or whatever), but this target will only work if you happen to specify a logging implementation as a root alongside it". And that takes away my ability to statically reason about a dependency graph - it adds extra context I need to know outside of my source code in order to correctly build this deployable.

We see this in the JVM world at the moment - people get really surprised when the resolve of their 3rdpary deps is different depending on what other targets they specified as roots. This API design is guiding people towards making that same mistake.

Ideally, imo, there would be one way of specifying dependencies (and maybe super-specific hooks for overrides, like the pytest rule has a flag for overriding the requirements there), and this feels like a _really_ general hammer which takes away a pretty simple one-way-to-do-something guarantee.

In the elastic case we need to be careful about preserving the order that we assign ranks after their processes have been started.  This is because we need to ensure that the oldest worker is always assigned rank 0 (for the purpose of sending the most up-to-date state to the new workers).

So one way we might be able to achieve this would be to track the timestamp that the host was first assigned, and then use that as the first sorting dimension.  So all hosts that were assigned together initially will then be ordered as a subgroup by number of slots.

See the commit https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/pull/9284/commits/8f3295530d0f4a209bf5744a6e0560b6a09eaa21. Originally, we more correctly modeled this as `is-a` by requiring that every class define a `PluginField` like `PythonLibraryField`. But, this both resulted in lots of boilerplate and was confusing, as Daniel first pointed out in https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/pull/9284#discussion_r391805164.

I'm willing to go back to having `PluginField`, but don't think it's worth it. Beyond extra boilerplate, it forces target authors to consider the concept of additional fields being added, even though they are by definition never going to consume those extra fields in their rules. (They wouldn't know at the time of writing the rules that those fields exist, so they wouldn't know to consume them). Instead, now, all targets work automatically with plugin fields for no additional work.

We could do some type of generated field, like `HaskellTarget.PluginField`, but that seems even more confusing to me because it's not discoverable and it could trip up MyPy (not certain).

Both of those names are intended to mean "all of the capabilities that exist".  My mistake was thinking that any application using this library would naturally support all of those capabilities -- that's definitely not the case, as here where *sometimes* tc-worker-runner doesn't support `"shutdown"`.

So I'm thinking that the simplest to understand would be to default to no capabilities and require the user of the library to "Add" every capability they do support to the protocol, before starting it.

Here's the logic. Each UserTopicPractice instance keeps track of one topic for one user. question_name is the last question practiced on that topic; when the topic is practiced it will go on to the next question associated with that topic.  (Or maybe it's encoding the next one that should be asked, and when the topic is practiced, it is set to be the next one to practice. Either way, it's the same for our purposes...)

When we *create* a UserTopicPractice, we need to set question_name to something. So the code sets it to be the name of the first question associated with the topic. This functino isn't called when we do that.

When we *delete* a UserTopicPractice for a topic, we use this function to look up the dbid of the row, then we delete that row_id.

This function doesn't seem to be referenced anywhere else in the repo. 

If we do end up moving the practice tool over at some point, it could make use of this function. But it will also want a version without the question_name in the query. Because it will be looking up the user, chapter, and sub-chapter, in order to find out what the curent question_name is.

Right, we only want to filter out those dfs that were empty originally and not those that produced an empty result, fixed that by adding the `skip_on_aligning` flag at the groupby kernel.

We have to filter out those frames that were empty originally, because they produce incorrect columns that shouldn't be in the result at all:

```python
>>> df
   a  b
0  1  3
1  1  4
2  2  5
3  2  6
>>> def func(group):
...     return (
...         pandas.DataFrame([[1, 2]], columns=["new1", "new2"])
...         if group.iloc[0, 0] == 1 else 
...         pandas.DataFrame(columns=["new2", "new3", "new4"]) # here we're producing an empty result
...     )
...
>>> df.groupby("a").apply(func) 
     new1 new2 new3 new4 # the columns of the empty result are joined to the non-empty result
1 0   1.0    2  NaN  NaN
>>> df.iloc[:0].groupby("a").apply(func) # runing groupby on an empty frame
Empty DataFrame
Columns: [a, b] # it produces columns that shouldn't be in the resulted frame, so we have to skip it when aligning
Index: []
```

```
Goexit terminates the goroutine that calls it. No other goroutine is affected. Goexit runs all deferred calls before terminating the goroutine. Because Goexit is not a panic, any recover calls in those deferred functions will return nil.

Calling Goexit from the main goroutine terminates that goroutine without func main returning. Since func main has not returned, the program continues execution of other goroutines. If all other goroutines exit, the program crashes
```

MeshCoP::TcatAgent encapsulates the TCAT protocol and state. It may be reused by multiple link layers which could coexist. Therefore the class Ble::BleSecure has its own instance of MeshCoP::TcatAgent. Likewise another like layer technology may have its own instance of MeshCoP::TcatAgent. Therefore, it makes sense that the class Ble::BleSecure invokes TcatAgent::Start(...) inside otBleSecureTcatStart(...). 
If we were to add another TCAT link layer, such as Thread or Uart, we would start the respective TcatAgent instance with otMyOtherLinkSecureTcatStart(...).

An otTcat API certainly would make sense if we were to allow the C API user to create its own MeshCoP::TcatAgent instances for use with its own custom carrier Implementation. This is not easy because TcatAgent::Connected(...) expects a MeshCoP::Dtls reference as an abstraction of the secure carrier.  MeshCoP::Dtls is currently not exposing a C API either.
tcat.h only exposes enums and callback potentially shared between different TCAT carriers.  I suggest not adding a full otTcat API,  unless the user community badly requests custom TCAT carriers. 
I hope that makes sense.

I tried to use a `BytesIO` object and kept getting this error when using `json.dump`:

```
>           fp.write(chunk)
E           TypeError: a bytes-like object is required, not 'str'

/usr/local/opt/pyenv/versions/3.7.2/lib/python3.7/json/__init__.py:180: TypeError
```

In this case I think additional check is not needed. Logic is as follows:
- `process_stashed_out_of_order_commits` is already scheduled to run in future
- catchup is triggered
- during `runFor` catch-up progresses as far as it can and stops
- `runFor` runs long enough so that scheduled action fires

It is possible to implement this in a more robust way (but at the cost of being more tied to node internals) by doing following things:
- set `process_stashed_out_of_order_commits` timeout to "infinity"
- start catchup
- wait until catchup enters expected stage
- manually fire `process_stashed_out_of_order_commits`
- remove catchup delayer and let it progress further

I see your point here and mainly agree to it. `NaN` values should be reported. 

- If gradients are really small `tf.clip_by_global_norm()` returns the original gradients and the vanishing gradient problem should be handled with corresponding methods 

- If gradients are exploding the clipping by global norm generally helps, but

- If we have (1) somewhere an `NaN` or `Inf` value in the gradients or (2) the global norm becomes `NaN` or `Inf`, the clipped gradients all become `NaN`. The safe-guard should avoid this by setting the gradients then to zero.

The last case is especially frustrating/costly as then the whole network turns useless and training has to be aborted. Usually an error like [here](https://discuss.ray.io/t/action-masking-error/8347/8) occurs and is uninformative. 

We could for the last case error out with a useful user message instead of silently safe-guarding. We could error out also before clipping, if any gradient is non-finite. I am interested in your opinion.

This is what I was talking about on Friday. CuPy's `asarray` expects the input to be in main memory, but we want it to be like `numpy.asarray` except expect the input to be on the GPU (to get a lot of already-written code to work with a substitution of libraries). The NumPy and CuPy libraries aren't symmetric in that particular way, so we have these classes to act as indirection between what the libraries actually do and what we need them to do. We need `asarray` to be `to_cupy` for GPU-bound Awkward arrays, so I just overloaded that behavior here. There might be a few more subtleties, but we can fix them as they come up because we have this indirection layer.

It would be cleaner if we keep transport code independent from the data being transferred. That would involve a bit of refactoring in the protocol engine, though.

[Here](https://github.com/etingof/pysnmp/blob/acd16a65f89b0165b881c4f33ee5802607583c3f/pysnmp/entity/engine.py#L149) is where the read message goes from transport/networking code into the protocol engine. I am thinking if we make `receiveMessage` returning unused bytes (as returned by the underlying pyasn1 decoder) and "unread" them [back](https://github.com/etingof/pysnmp/blob/master/pysnmp/carrier/asyncore/dgram/base.py#L163) into some internal queue like [__inQueue](https://github.com/etingof/pysnmp/blob/master/pysnmp/carrier/asyncore/dgram/base.py#L34), but (in case of stream transport) it should probably just a (growing/shrinking) string rather than a list...? Just like you have it in `remaining_bytes`.

That would let us remove this header finding heuristics completely. And, most importantly, make searching way more reliable.

One of the interesting problems along this way would be to detect and eventually reset the growing `inQueue` if decoder repeatedly fails to decode it. May be this could be based on a reasonable SNMP message size, like if the buffer grows beyond 4K - drop it.

The `DEFAULT_CONFIG` is used every time we call `create_dataset` without the `config` parameter:

https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/c00c4d36d07552dbe0cc4fd399a9bf911bf99fcb/test/datasets_utils.py#L311-L314

If a config is specified, we overwrite the default config. This is what happens in every built-in test that uses the `@test_all_configs` decorator:

https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/c00c4d36d07552dbe0cc4fd399a9bf911bf99fcb/test/datasets_utils.py#L435-L437

But there are also tests that do not specify a config, which means the `DEFAULT_CONFIG` is used as is:

https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/c00c4d36d07552dbe0cc4fd399a9bf911bf99fcb/test/datasets_utils.py#L431-L433

---

The `create_dataset()` method does not simply do

```python
if config is None:
     config = cls.DEFAULT_CONFIG.copy()
```

because we want to make sure, that we always get a "complete" config in `dataset_args` and `inject_fake_data`. 

Imagine a dataset with multiple keyword arguments. We could obtain a complete config by checking all available combinations. Since the number of tests would grow quadratic with the number of possible values for the arguments this should not be they way to go. Thus, we populate the `DEFAULT_CONFIG` with complete set of parameters and only update the ones the user wants to test.

> Parsing them out of texty labels is the only thing that seems annoying.

I suppose my tastes have been warped by matching records based on hashes! I was not imagining that people would _parse_ them out of texty labels; I expected people to (magically?) know what the semantic values were and use those semantic values to recreate the relevant texty label...

As in, here, we would the expect that whoever called `summarize_joint_samples_batch` would know what quantiles were used, and could recreate the `'rel_uplift_{}'.format(q)` (yes we would need to make this more convenient if we go with the current design). I guess the deep question is "can/should the columns have labels that tell the caller everything they need to know about how to treat the data in that column?" and I'm not convinced the answer is "yes" - because for the quantiles the caller needs to know what quantile it is (a float) and what role it plays in the analysis (is it a CI? is it one of probability bands, and if so, which?). If the caller can't infer everything from the labels, then they have to already store some information about what labels they expect to be in the output, and so this label-reconstruction approach is less limiting.

So: yes, it's a bit annoying, but it shouldn't have architectural implications and therefore _might_ be the kind of thing you get over quickly?

> I'm tripping over the flat indexing scheme, since it feels like we "really" have a handful of different kinds of numbers rather than a single collection of numbers.

Follow me down the slippery slope: wouldn't it be nice if the columns were a multi-index, where the highest level labels are `['relative_uplift', 'absolute_uplift', 'max_abs_diff', 'prob_win']`, and all but `'prob_win'` have a lower level label per quantile value. Yes the highest level of the index doesn't have an important order, but overall the DataFrame's columns do fall into groups and within each group there is an ordering. This nicely separates the collections of numbers and allows you to address one collection at a time.

But multiindexes are awkward/fiddly and don't offer much over a flattened index, other than aesthetics. If you're going to address each column of data by its label, then maybe it's not the end of the world if you carry around some extra columns - as in if your function `plot_absolute_uplift_with_error_bars()` already needs to know the labels of the columns with relevant data, perhaps it's not atrocious if the data object also happens to have a bunch of columns about relative uplift that go unused?

The other aspect I like about having just one DataFrame here is that every column shares an index. Indexes will eventually represent one of a few different dimensions (multi-output `stat_fn`, thresholds, points in time that the analysis window starts); IMO it's nice to know that the `relative_uplift` and `absolute_uplift` objects implicitly share an index.

I think this is a situation where both sides have strengths and weaknesses; if you consider these issues and decide that an extra layer of data structure will make downstream use easier/neater/safer, then sure, we can stick these in a `dict` (or an `attr` class)

Unfortunately, we don't have an independent source of truth for the gradients the way we do for the PDF itself.  We check our symbolic forward against the densities given by scipy, but scipy doesn't have functions for analytic gradients of the PDFs, just the same kinds of tools for numeric differentiation that we've got, so there's nothing for `check_symbolic_backward` to check against.

Similarly, we've found that the numeric gradient is most reliable in float64.  The closed-form gradient functions we've written are -- assuming they're written correctly at all -- more accurate than numeric approximation can be.  Checking the gradient numerically in float64 provides a lot of evidence about whether the gradient functions are written correctly, but checking float32 doesn't add very much more; discrepancies are more likely to be caused by numeric errors in the approximation than in our code.

Regular invokes accept an `InvokeOptions` which allows specifying the provider and version.

Having a generated method accept `InvokeOptions` as an argument felt unnatural to me since, if you wanted to specify a custom provider, you would have done so when creating the resource itself.

So I've made it so that the provider / version is picked up from the resource, which is passed into this function as part of the generated SDK code, e.g.:

```python
    def get_kubeconfig(__self__,
                       profile_name: Optional[str] = None,
                       role_arn: Optional[str] = None) -> pulumi.Output['Cluster.GetKubeconfigResult']:
        __args__ = dict()
        __args__['__self__'] = __self__
        __args__['profileName'] = profile_name
        __args__['roleArn'] = role_arn
        return pulumi.runtime.call('eks:index:Cluster/getKubeconfig', __args__, res=__self__, typ=Cluster.GetKubeconfigResult)
```

There are two cases that I need to handle in subsequent changes:

1. If we are going to use `Call` for leave functions that accept inputs / create resources, we will want to support `InvokeOptions` for that, so I'll need to update this function to be able to pass either a resource or `InvokeOptions` to specify the custom provider.

2. If the instance of the resource was rehydrated from a resource reference, I don't think the `res._provider` or `res._version` will be available to us. For that case, we'll need to make sure we can fill in that information from the built-in `pulumi:pulumi:getResource`.

```python
module = __import__(module_name, globals(), locals(), [class_name], 0)
subsystem_class = getattr(module, class_name)
```

Basically, by using `_import__()` directly, we can do the equivalent of a `from <module_name> import <class_name>`. That distinction seems like it would only be relevant for performance reasons, and I don't think importing a single module is an optimization we need to worry about right now.

One concern I had initially is that the implementation details of the fuzz logger (e.g., when data should be written) were being leaked outside the class. However, once I realized that it essentially signals completion of a test case, it made more sense. Here are some readability/usability suggestions:

1. Rename the method to `end_test_case`.
2. Add an `end_test` method.
3. Add these methods to ifuzz_logger.py, and to each of the fuzz logger implementation (text, csv) for consistency. They can do nothing or log a simple message in those cases.
4. Within `fuzz_logger_db.py`, rename `write_log` to `_write_log`. The `end_test_case` method will call `_write_log` without force. The `end_test` method will call it with force.

This allows us to keep all content regarding database optimization strictly within the `FuzzLoggerDb` class. It will also make the code within `FuzzLoggerDb` more readable to a newcomer, as the public methods will make clear how and when `_write_log` is called.

The way it works is that both `Job` and `JobDescription` have `cores` and friends as properties. `JobDescription` stores the actual values, and `Job` fulfills all reads and writes by sending them along to the associated `JobDescription`.

The values come in initially through the `__init__` of Job, and they're passed along to the JobDescription when it is created.

Note that descriptionClass is a class, *not* a JobDescription instance. Job is responsible for creating its own JobDescription. It's just that some Job descendants need to use JobDescription descendants as their descriptions, and the best way I thought of to do that was to pass the type along to Job's `__init__`. Otherwise all the name determining and requirements munging would have to move to methods that all the Job descendants would have to call in their `__init__` methods before constructing their descriptions.

But I took a cursory look into a different solution and, as far as I can tell, I don't think it is possible to suppress the warning for a specific argument using the existing functions:

- The `unused-argument` warning is generated [in `_check_unused_arguments`](https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint/blob/97f4f2ae187df933f072d74fd8347ec14213f5de/pylint/checkers/variables.py#L1616), which is called by `_check_is_unused`, which in turn is called by `VariablesChecker.leave_functiondef`.
  - So unfortunately there is no `VariablesChecker.leave_argumentdef` or something we could hook into and suppress the warning for specific arguments. The suppression has to happen for function definitions.
- `suppress_message` could be another possible point for suppressing the warning for specific arguments
   - Unfortunately there is no way to pass additional information to the [internal suppression logic](https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint-plugin-utils/blob/db903068d4ad64d919c014af097cdafadcf1748d/pylint_plugin_utils/__init__.py#L82). It only uses the name of the warning.
      - But the `to_append_args` used in the internal logic contains the name of the argument of `unused-argument` warnings.
   - Extending the `suppress_message` of `pylint-plugin-utils` to allow suppression of warnings based on additional information could be cleaner way to implement this.

You're right, limiting deltas to max_depth is a problem if you want the book to be as big as possible. However, `max_depth` does not filter the deltas being sent to downstream consumers. That means we do need to do this filtering somewhere, otherwise a downstream consumer with max_depth=20 would see deltas for depth=38 for instance (because we fetch a snapshot of 100 depths minimum). In fact this problem exists without the snapshots as well, because the web socket could get a book update orders higher on the order book.

I'm not immediately sure how to fix this in the most elegant way. You could either change the cause to be `if i < max_depth or not self.max_depth` but that doesn't feel very nice and only fixes the issue for snapshots, not for book updates. Alternatively you could filter the deltas in the callback if max_depth is set, but that's very inefficient and rather cumbersome. @bmoscon what do you think?

## use choices= to give the parser better docs

Add `choices=[probe.lower() for probe in IsHealthyProbe._NAMES_TO_VALUES]` so that the help formatter can print out the available options to users and also forces validation into the argparse code instead of ours. 

## rely on argparse doing validation

With that, you can drop `_convert_probes` and make the for loop on L92 look like:

```python
for probe in args.probes:
    checker(args.endpoint, IsHealthyProbe._NAMES_TO_VALUES[probe.upper()])
```

## make it safer to do the arguments in any order

I think making it one multi-argument flag like this may make parsing kinda hard. If you do `--probes` before the two positional arguments (which on its face seems reasonable) it makes the parser not understand the positional arguments:

```
ubuntu@reddit:~/src/baseplate.py (baseplate.py)$ baseplate-healthcheck --probes readiness thrift localhost:9002
usage: baseplate-healthcheck [-h]
                             [--probes {readiness,liveness,startup} [{readiness,liveness,startup} ...]]
                             {thrift,wsgi} endpoint
baseplate-healthcheck: error: argument --probes: invalid choice: 'thrift' (choose from 'readiness', 'liveness', 'startup')
ubuntu@reddit:~/src/baseplate.py (baseplate.py)$ baseplate-healthcheck thrift localhost:9002 --probes readiness
OK!
```

> What are the advantages of making the code change on line 8?

The result of `'branch 0v'.split()` is exactly `['branch', '0v']`. In using the actual list, you're saving a few steps ([i.e.](https://www.merriam-webster.com/video/ie-vs-eg) calling a function that would split the string and return a list).

> Just looking at the code here on the PR and not in my editor, what I _think_ you're saying is that we can use a list comprehension to build a list of actions to take based on what the character after the erroneous `0`.

Yes. More precisely, that is a generator expression. It's better to use them in such cases as it stops at the first element found. The action would be the same for any of the characters after `0`, though: replace `0` with `-`.

> [...] Then I think you're saying that I can use the memoize annotation (is that what they're called in Python) to return the pre-defined "correct" and corrective action to take, thus allowing this functionality to be expanded upon bit by bit in future PRs.

You're partially correct regarding the memoization. There's a pattern for it in the project, as you can see in the link I posted. Let's take it as an example:

https://github.com/nvbn/thefuck/blob/365db1ee41d7bcd9f62d910f3b20361ea3499d92/thefuck/rules/git_add.py#L5-L27

Note that the `_get_missing_file` function is used in both `match` (on line 20) and `get_new_command` (on line 25). These are two separate *“phases”* of the *“correction”* process. TheFuck first tries to match a rule. If that rule matches, it then gets the new command. That means `_get_missing_file` is called two times. That's when the memoization comes in handy.

ps.: thinking over it again, maybe that's also a "Nit" if you consider the fact that the proposed function wouldn't be much expensive.

> I don't think we send an RA with lifetime zero to remove ours and basically expect the timeout to invalidate it

The `newOnLinkPrefix` is default to `nullptr` and it will be used for `SendRouterAdvertisement()` to send RA with zero lifetime. I may be more readable to have `SendRouterAdvertisement` together with `RemoveExternalRoute` but we didn't do it so that we can send all RA options in a single RA message.

But the issue still exists, per https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc4862#section-5.5.3:
>1.  If the received Valid Lifetime is greater than 2 hours or
       greater than RemainingLifetime, set the valid lifetime of the
       corresponding address to the advertised Valid Lifetime.
>2.  If RemainingLifetime is less than or equal to 2 hours, ignore
       the Prefix Information option with regards to the valid
       lifetime, unless the Router Advertisement from which this
       option was obtained has been authenticated (e.g., via Secure
       Neighbor Discovery [RFC3971]).  If the Router Advertisement
       was authenticated, the valid lifetime of the corresponding
       address should be set to the Valid Lifetime in the received
       option.
>
>3.  Otherwise, reset the valid lifetime of the corresponding
          address to 2 hours.

SLAAC address will be retained even when RA with zero valid lifetime is received (zero valid lifetime is useful to tell other BRs to start advertising their own prefixes). My idea is to apply the same rule of SLAAC to external routes and delay removing external routes before the SLAAC addresses are actually expired. Thoughts?

The idea is that when a client is stopped and restarted again any previously registered services would register again (as if they are being added again). Or if we are in middle of removing a service, we mark it to be removed once client is re-started. The reason that we need this, is that the user may `Start` with a possibly new SRP server.

We also have `Pause` mechanism which we use internally when device is disconnected/offline. For now, we do not expose the `Pause` as an external API since I don't think there is any immediate use for it (we can add later if there is a use for it). 

The main difference between `Stop()` and `Pause()` is that on `Pause` we keep the previously registered services as they are (i.e. we won't re-add/re-register them on `Resume`).

When semaphore is created it requires running event loop. If there is none, one is created.

If not being careful it can quickly happen that the semaphore is bound to a different event loop than the one that is used in listener. See 

https://stackoverflow.com/questions/55918048/asyncio-semaphore-runtimeerror-task-got-future-attached-to-a-different-loop

I just thought it is nicer to create semaphore when it is needed and avoid troubles with different event loops no matter how listener was initialized.

I tried out removing `asbool` from `_logger.py` and this continues to happen.

This error also happens if these are the env vars used: `DD_LOGS_INJECTION=false DD_TRACE_DEBUG=true DD_CALL_BASIC_CONFIG=true`, meaning the problem is on the log injection setting, not call basic config or `_logger.py`.

When log injection is set, **tracer.py** calls `patch(logging=true)`: https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-py/blob/1.x/ddtrace/tracer.py#L79 . 

`logging/patch.py` calls on `ddtrace.tracer` if there's no `tracer` yet: https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-py/blob/1.x/ddtrace/contrib/logging/patch.py#L41 .

However, at that point, there isn't a `tracer` yet, which explains my earlier results:

```
dir(ddtrace)

I am not suggesting to use `otPlatExternalHeapAlloc` method directly in core. 

Currently, the user (posix) code has to set up function pointers using `otHeapSetCAllocFree`. 
```cpp
#if OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_HEAP_EXTERNAL_ENABLE
    otHeapSetCAllocFree(calloc, free);
#endif
```

I am suggesting that we should define two more platform methods (i.e. otPlatExternalHeapAlloc and otPlatExternalHeapAFree) and platforms supporting external heap (e.g. POSIX) should define these two methods. Then, OT core is can freely call `Instance::HeapCAlloc` in constructors no matter it's using internal heap or external.

I think both @jianoaix and I are pushing for hyphen/underscore separators since this field will be used in dashboard queries, since it's less ambiguous on how different systems will tokenize the query. E.g. will "Data tests" translate to a single EQUALS/CONTAINS query on a "Data tests" token, or an OR query for "Data" and "tests" tokens?

When determining the separator to use in a manifest field string, I usually go with:
1. If only read by humans, go with more readable space separator.
2. If only used programmatically, go with less ambiguous hyphen/underscore separator.
3. If used by both, go with (2).

i'm excited to offer something like a task_definition_kwargs field or something that lets you specify arbitrary task definition config so that we don't have to re-implement the whole spec as Dagster config, but I don't think it would be sufficient here. The pieces config that we're offering are a mixture of things on the dagster container (log configuration / sidecars), the task definition (execution_role_arn, task_role_arn), and the task (cluster / security groups / subnets).

Generally I'm trying to follow the general playbook here (https://elementl.quip.com/9K0bAWhc6t9n/Agent-Configuration-Options) - where we have a relatively simple and blessed way to set the most common pieces of config we think people are likely to want(secrets etc.) and then eventually also an escape hatch where we expose the full raw config for the power users. I think there's value in not needing to send people to the boto spec if they want to do something like 'set env vars' or 'point at an existing cluster' or 'change the IAM role that's used'. But which things are considered 'the most common pieces of config' is very subjective.

@RehanSD I happened to be thinking about this some more, and I think we should go with your solution (2) for now. I have a few reasons.

a) Serialization/deserialization is really expensive, but the concat is not terribly so. This optimization would save on the concat, but would not save any serialization/deserialization. Even for this 2 GB pandas dataframe, pandas takes 2.4 sec to sort the entire dataframe by 1 column, but only .103 sec to concatenate one row of partitions:

```python
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

num_axis_partitions = int(2**4)
partitions = [[pd.DataFrame(np.random.randint(0,100,(2**16,2**4))) for _ in range(num_axis_partitions)] for _ in range(num_axis_partitions)]
%time first_row = pd.concat(partitions[0], axis=1)
df = pd.concat([pd.concat([p for p in row], axis=1, ignore_index=True) for row in partitions], ignore_index=True)
%time sorted = df.sort_values(0)
```

b) If you want to cut out the time for the extra concat, I think a more natural solution is to have the virtual partition `apply` method update its list of blocks to only contain the concatenated dataframe. We should do that in a separate PR. But this is something we should think about after we've profiled performance and observed that the `concat` is taking a long time.
c) in the very long term (maybe 6 months from now), we should be pipelining these steps anyway, so 
d) I am still uncomfortable with solution (4) because I think it's kind of awkward and it would only be useful for this use case.

I've added a docstring that may shed some light on this. We actually explicitly order by checkpoint ID in the last tuple entry. The logic is as follows:

`_get_checkpoint_score` returns a tuple `(is_not_na, metric, id)`

Higher values are preferred/kept.

`sort()` first sorts by the first tuple index, then the second, then the third.

The first index ensures that NaN metrics are discarded first (rated worst).

The second index then sorts by actual metric.

If the metrics are the same (e.g. when no attr is defined --> we get -inf), then we sort by checkpoint ID and keep the later checkpoints.

Since I was the one bringing up RVO and whether it works...

```asm
; GCC
.L32:
        mov     rax, QWORD PTR [rsp]
        mov     QWORD PTR [r13+0], rax
        mov     rax, QWORD PTR [rsp+8]
        mov     QWORD PTR [r13+8], rax
        mov     rax, QWORD PTR [rsp+16]
        mov     QWORD PTR [r13+16], rax
.L24:
        mov     rax, r13
```

In other words, after populating the vector, we move 3 words, from the top of the stack into `R13` and return a little later. This is what a move looks like. At O3 it will be further optimized to 128bit move + 64bit move, instead of 3 x 64bit moves.

```asm
; Clang
        mov     rax, r14
```

You guessed it. RVO works here.

```asm
; MSVC
$LN3@to_vector:
        mov     rax, QWORD PTR vec$1[rsp]
        mov     QWORD PTR [rsi], rax
        mov     QWORD PTR [rsi+8], rcx
        mov     rax, QWORD PTR vec$1[rsp+16]
        mov     QWORD PTR [rsi+16], rax
$LN36@to_vector:
        mov     rax, rsi
```

This is the exact same thing as GCC, just with different registers. Except that I couldn't get MSVC to use XMM0 and perform a 128bit move.

 

The branch with the temporary gets optimized to a `memcpy` and RVO is used. The assembly is just beautiful in this case.

But that exception appearing or not depends on which server you choose to run your app.  The response here is informative only.  Starlette is ASGI toolkit aimed at simplifying development.
As I see it, here are the options:
1) just close the connection().  This is always supported and is the original way of rejecting a connection.  Server provides a 403 response.
2) Close the response, but proide additional information for those interested.
  2a) Server supports the extension, and a response body is delivered along with the message.
  2b) Server does not support the response and body is ignored.
  
It is th case 2b) which worries you.  IMHO, the response body is informative only.  Depending on web server a response body can or can not be delivered.   If the information is somehow protocol critical (not recommended), then the application can always check for the presence of the extension.

Ok, how about this:  Instead of an exception, how about a warning?  This will get flagged to the developer but it won't crash your application if an extension isn't supported.   And we won't have to push the burden on the developer to always check the state for the presence of the extension before using a close call with an extended api?

I think that the PPO algorithm has to trust its framework-specific Learner subclasses to do the right thing (PPO-wise) in this one `additional_update` call.

Also, in this particular case (updating the entropy coeff), it really doesn't make sense to have more control. There is no reason to NOT update this coeff every single iteration.

I do see that this situation is different for say target net updates, where you don't want these updates to happen every iteration. So for these, we should indeed have a flag (rather than deciding inside additional_update by the `timestep` given as the algorithm should be the one deciding WHEN to do WHAT).

Bottom line: Yes, for target net updates, this makes sense. For lr/entropy/kl coeff updates not so much.

ok then it's not an issue with `read()`. You can simply write something like 
```python
def get_transform(datasets: Sequence[str], target_aligned_pixels: bool = False):

    # scan input files
    xs = []
    ys = []
    resolutions = []
    for dataset in datasets:
        with rasterio.open(dataset) as src:
            left, bottom, right, top = src.bounds
            resolutions.extend(src.res)
        xs.extend([left, right])
        ys.extend([bottom, top])

    dst_w, dst_s, dst_e, dst_n = min(xs), min(ys), max(xs), max(ys)

    # Select the first resolution (this could be configurable)
    res = resolutions[0]


    if target_aligned_pixels:
        dst_w = math.floor(dst_w / res[0]) * res[0]
        dst_e = math.ceil(dst_e / res[0]) * res[0]
        dst_s = math.floor(dst_s / res[1]) * res[1]
        dst_n = math.ceil(dst_n / res[1]) * res[1]

    # Compute output array shape. We guarantee it will cover the output
    # bounds completely
    output_width = int(round((dst_e - dst_w) / res[0]))
    output_height = int(round((dst_n - dst_s) / res[1]))

    output_transform = Affine.translation(dst_w, dst_n) * Affine.scale(res[0], -res[1])
    return output_transform
```

But if we _want_ to do that is another question. The precedent was set with isort to not, as the location does change things. For example, our old `contrib/.isort.cfg` only applied to `contrib/**` it would be very wrong for Pants to do something magical. Also with shfmt, Pytest, and Shellcheck, the location does matter and you can set in subdirectories so that only part of your code uses the config. 

So, doing the relocation would make a tradeoff to allow people to consolidate config files in one place, but break the workflow where you specifically placed the config in a subdirectory so that only some files use it. Perhaps the solution is to offer both via some mechanism like a second option or string prefix - but no users have asked for that relocation yet, so I'd bias towards keeping this.

https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/28545a0bfd5586dcf2de8162cd4af9bfaa72f7f6/sdk/resources/azure-mgmt-resource/azure/mgmt/resource/subscriptions/_subscription_client.py#L76

Then it is used as the `http_logging_policy` attribute of the `PipelineClient`:

https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/227c700c3bae19a8f17327a3901ebd6cd5ac59f6/sdk/core/azure-mgmt-core/azure/mgmt/core/_pipeline_client.py#L61-L62

So the priority/flow here is more like kwargs > config > default one.

Another thing that should be done in the tests (new constraint): after each value test, such as

```python
    assert ak.to_list(listoffsetarray.rpad_and_clip(3, 0)) == [
        [0.0, 1.1, 2.2],
        [],
        [3.3, 4.4],
    ]
```

we now need to do a type-tracer test. The equivalent for the above is

```python
    assert (
        listoffsetarray.typetracer.rpad_and_clip(3, 0).form
        == listoffsetarray.rpad_and_clip(3, 0).form
    )
```

The first part, `listoffsetarray.typetracer`, makes an equivalent of `listoffsetarray` with non-concrete arrays in place of the NumPy arrays. We want to pass this type-tracer version of the Content through the same operation that we pass the Content through, then verify that they both have the same Form. That ensures that it will be possible to Daskify this operation, and that users will be able to work with lazy data with full type information. (Note: I've usually added these to tests with a keyboard macro—an imperfect one that I run and then fix the errors, which is faster than typing out the whole thing manually each time.)

The type-tracer test is different from the ones you've commented out: the ones you've commented out seem to be verifying that the type of padded data is equal to the type of the original data + option type (because now `None` is a possible value). That test is specific to what rpad does; the type-tracer tests are general and we have to apply them to all operations.

Yeah, that's where I started. Obviously that only works when you also opt in to using the `distutils` that comes with `setuptools`, but that's fine.

The bigger issue is that the code later in this function tries to strip off the suffix later, which is not possible to do if `get_ext_filename` uses anything other than `get_condig_var('EXT_SUFFIX')` (at least without the same logic as what I added here anyway).

Adding the hook in `setuptools._distutils.sysconfig.get_config_var` would have made some sense. However, I assume at some point in the future that the patches for `build_ext` will eventually merge _somewhere_, and at some point `distutils.sysconfig` will be replaced with the standard library's `sysconfig` module. If the hook were in `_distutils`, there's a pretty good chance it would be forgotten later and the functionality would be lost. This way, there's no chance of it being forgotten in any refactorings.

My intention was to handle the Tag formatting for values in my If function.  Basically, I wanted to be lazy and write `Tag('key', 'value')` instead of `{'Key': 'key', 'Value': 'value'}` as the True and False values for my If statement.  I have an example in the test_TagConditional` method.

I tried initially to reuse the Tags class, however that resulted in each value being a list instead of just the single tag.  Basically, this:

```
Tags(
    {'foo': 'foo'},
    If('MyCondition', Tags('bar', 'bar'), Tags('baz', 'baz'))
)
```

resulted in 

```
[
  {
    "Fn::If": [
       "MyCondition",
       [{"Key": "bar", "Value": "bar"}]
       [{"Key": "baz", "Value": "baz"}]
    ]
  },
  'Value': 'foo', 'Key': 'foo'}
]
```

Just setting the data value seemed simpler than wrapping\extending the Tags class and then writing some logic to only convert the first value in the underlying list, but I can update to that method if desired.

Although not heavily tested and not yet documented, the `GW_TESTS_USE_EXTERNAL_TASKCLUSTER` env var was intended to be used as a means to be able to select whether tests should run against mocks (for speed) or against a real deployment, for integration testing purposes. If tests create objects outside of the API interface, they would not work if run against a real deployment. Since an object can be created via the API, would it be possible for the tests to use that instead, so that the tests could still be run against a real deployment? My thinking is that we could run tests against a real deployment when making a release, to give us further certainty that things function end-to-end (e.g. this may have helped protect against the taskQueueId failures that we introduced recently, that didn't get picked up by the mocks when the task definition structure changed).

If you like, you could keep this convenience method, but in the body wrap the object creation API call rather than updating the underlying map directly, to preserve the capability.

Technically, return type should be `Union[torch.Tensor, F]` but this has no practical use. So if we would be calling this explicitly, we needed to `cast` anyway if we know we get a `Feature` type back. Good thing for us, we never call this directly. Thus, we can get away with the `Tensor` annotation, since all `Feature`'s are also `Tensor`'s.
